obj.content:
[center][b]Urban farming in Paris[/b][/center][br/]
Vertical tubes are used to grow strawberries, [blank]1[/blank] and herbs.[br/]
There will eventually be a daily harvest of as much as  [blank]2[/blank]  in weight of fruit and vegetables.[br/]
It may be possible that the farm`s produce will account for as much as 10% of the city`s  [blank]3[/blank] overall.
obj.html_content:
Urban farming in Paris


Vertical tubes are used to grow strawberries, and herbs.
There will eventually be a daily harvest of as much as in weight of fruit and vegetables.
It may be possible that the farm`s produce will account for as much as 10% of the city`s overall.

added_content.ex_information:
Complete the summary below.
Choose
NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS/OR A NUMBER
 from the passage for each answer.
Write your answers in boxes 1–3 on your answer sheet.

obj.title: Urban farming
obj.content:
[i]In Paris, urban farmers are trying a soil-free approach to agriculture that uses less space and fewer resources. Could it help cities face the threats to our food supplies?[/i][br/][br/]
On top of a striking new exhibition hall in southern Paris, the world's largest urban rooftop farm has started to bear fruit. Strawberries that are small, intensely flavoured and resplendently red sprout abundantly from large plastic tubes. Peer inside and you see the tubes are completely hollow, the roots of dozens of strawberry plants dangling down inside them. From identical vertical tubes nearby burst row upon row of lettuces; near those are aromatic herbs, such as basil, sage and peppermint. Opposite, in narrow, horizontal trays packed not with soil but with coconut fibre, grow cherry tomatoes, shiny aubergines and brightly coloured chards.[br/][br/]
Pascal Hardy, an engineer and sustainable development consultant, began experimenting with vertical farming and aeroponic growing towers – as the soil-free plastic tubes are known – on his Paris apartment block roof five years ago. The urban rooftop space above the exhibition hall is somewhat bigger: 14,000 square metres and almost exactly the size of a couple of football pitches. Already, the team of young urban farmers who tend it have picked, in one day, 3,000 lettuces and 150 punnets of strawberries. When the remaining two thirds of the vast open area are in production, 20 staff will harvest up to 1,000 kg of perhaps 35 different varieties of fruit and vegetables, every day. 'We're not ever, obviously, going to feed the whole city this way,' cautions Hardy. 'In the urban environment you're working with very significant practical constraints, clearly, on what you can do and where. But if enough unused space can be developed like this, there's no reason why you shouldn't eventually target maybe between 5% and 10% of consumption.'[br/][br/]
Perhaps most significantly, however, this is a real-life showcase for the work of Hardy's flourishing urban agriculture consultancy, Agripolis, which is currently fielding enquiries from around the world to design, build and equip a new breed of soil-free inner-city farm. 'The method's advantages are many,' he says. 'First, I don't much like the fact that most of the fruit and vegetables we eat have been treated with something like 17 different pesticides, or that the intensive farming techniques that produced them are such huge generators of greenhouse gases. I don't much like the fact, either, that they've travelled an average of 2,000 refrigerated kilometres to my plate, that their quality is so poor, because the varieties are selected for their capacity to withstand such substantial journeys, or that 80% of the price I pay goes to wholesalers and transport companies, not the producers.'[br/][br/]
Produce grown using this soil-free method, on the other hand – which relies solely on a small quantity of water, enriched with organic nutrients, pumped around a closed circuit of pipes, towers and trays – is 'produced up here, and sold locally, just down there. It barely travels at all,' Hardy says. 'You can select crop varieties for their flavour, not their resistance to the transport and storage chain, and you can pick them when they're really at their best, and not before.' No soil is exhausted, and the water that gently showers the plants' roots every 12 minutes is recycled, so the method uses 90% less water than a classic intensive farm for the same yield.[br/][br/]
Urban farming is not, of course, a new phenomenon. Inner-city agriculture is booming from Shanghai to Detroit and Tokyo to Bangkok.[br/][br/]
Strawberries are being grown in disused shipping containers, mushrooms in underground carparks. Aeroponic farming, he says, is 'virtuous'. The equipment weighs little, can be installed on almost any flat surface and is cheap to buy: roughly €100 to €150 per square metre. It is cheap to run, too, consuming a tiny fraction of the electricity used by some techniques.[br/][br/]
Produce grown this way typically sells at prices that, while generally higher than those of classic intensive agriculture, are lower than soil-based organic growers. There are limits to what farmers can grow this way, of course, and much of the produce is suited to the summer months. 'Root vegetables we cannot do, at least not yet,' he says. 'Radishes are OK, but carrots, potatoes, that kind of thing – the roots are simply too long. Fruit trees are obviously not an option. And beans tend to take up a lot of space for not much return.' Nevertheless, urban farming of the kind being practised in Paris is one part of a bigger and fast-changing picture that is bringing food production closer to our lives.[br/][br/]
obj.content_zh:
[i]在巴黎，城市农民正在尝试一种使用更少空间和资源的无土农业方法。它能帮助城市应对粮食供应面临的威胁吗？[/i][br/][br/]
在巴黎南部一座引人注目的新展览馆顶部，世界上最大的城市屋顶农场已经开始开花结果。小巧玲珑、味道浓郁、鲜红欲滴的草莓从大型塑料管中大量萌发。往里一看，管子完全是空心的，几十株草莓的根系垂在里面。旁边同样的垂直管子里，一排排莴苣破土而出；附近还有罗勒、鼠尾草和薄荷等芳香草本植物。对面狭窄的水平托盘里装的不是泥土，而是椰子纤维，里面种着樱桃番茄、闪亮的茄子和色彩鲜艳的甜菜。[br/][br/]
P帕斯卡尔-哈迪（Pascal Hardy）是一名工程师和可持续发展顾问，五年前他开始在自己的巴黎公寓楼屋顶上试验垂直耕作和气培种植塔（无土塑料管）。展览馆上方的城市屋顶空间更大一些：14,000 平方米，几乎相当于几个足球场大小。由城市青年农民组成的团队已经在一天之内采摘了 3000 株生菜和 150 蒲式耳草莓。当大片空地的其余三分之二投入生产后，20 名员工每天将收获多达 1000 公斤、约 35 个不同品种的水果和蔬菜。哈迪警告说："显然，我们不可能用这种方式养活整个城市。在城市环境中，你所能做的、能在哪里做，显然都受到很大的实际限制。但如果能像这样开发足够多的闲置空间，就没有理由不最终将目标锁定在 5%-10%的消耗量上。[br/][br/]
不过，最重要的是，这可能是哈迪的城市农业咨询公司 Agripolis 工作的真实展示，该公司目前正在接受来自世界各地的咨询，要求设计、建造和装备新型的无土市内农场。这种方法有很多优点，"他说。首先，我不太喜欢我们吃的大多数水果和蔬菜都经过大约 17 种不同杀虫剂的处理，也不太喜欢生产这些水果和蔬菜的集约化耕作技术会产生大量温室气体。我也不喜欢这些水果和蔬菜平均要经过 2000 公里的冷藏运输才能送到我的餐桌上，我也不喜欢它们的质量如此之差，因为这些品种是为了能够承受如此长途的运输而被挑选出来的，我也不喜欢我所支付的价格中有 80% 是批发商和运输公司的，而不是生产商的。[br/][br/]
另一方面，使用这种无土栽培法种植的农产品--仅靠少量富含有机养分的水在管道、塔架和托盘组成的闭合回路中循环--"在这里生产，在当地销售，就在下面。哈迪说："这些水几乎不经过任何运输。你可以根据作物的风味，而不是它们对运输和储存链的耐受性来选择作物品种，你可以在它们真正达到最佳状态时采摘，而不是在采摘之前。这种方法不会耗尽土壤，而且每 12 分钟轻轻喷洒在植物根部的水都会被循环利用，因此在产量相同的情况下，这种方法比传统的集约型农场少用 90% 的水。[br/][br/]
当然，城市农业并非新现象。从上海到底特律，从东京到曼谷，城市内的农业都在蓬勃发展。[br/][br/]
人们在废弃的集装箱里种植草莓，在地下停车场种植蘑菇。他说，气生栽培是 “良性 ”的。设备重量小，几乎可以安装在任何平面上，而且购买成本低：每平方米大约 100 至 150 欧元。它的运行成本也很低，耗电量仅为某些技术的一小部分。[br/][br/]
以这种方式种植的农产品的售价通常高于传统的集约型农业，但低于以土壤为基础的有机种植者。当然，农民用这种方式种植的作物也有局限性，大部分农产品都适合在夏季种植。他说："我们不能种植根茎类蔬菜，至少现在还不行。萝卜还可以，但胡萝卜、土豆之类的根太长了。果树显然也不行。而豆类往往占用大量空间，收益却不高。尽管如此，在巴黎开展的这种城市耕作只是快速变化的大环境中的一部分，它让粮食生产更加贴近我们的生活。
obj.html_content:
In Paris, urban farmers are trying a soil-free approach to agriculture that uses less space and fewer resources. Could it help cities face the threats to our food supplies?



On top of a striking new exhibition hall in southern Paris, the world's largest urban rooftop farm has started to bear fruit. Strawberries that are small, intensely flavoured and resplendently red sprout abundantly from large plastic tubes. Peer inside and you see the tubes are completely hollow, the roots of dozens of strawberry plants dangling down inside them. From identical vertical tubes nearby burst row upon row of lettuces; near those are aromatic herbs, such as basil, sage and peppermint. Opposite, in narrow, horizontal trays packed not with soil but with coconut fibre, grow cherry tomatoes, shiny aubergines and brightly coloured chards.



Pascal Hardy, an engineer and sustainable development consultant, began experimenting with vertical farming and aeroponic growing towers – as the soil-free plastic tubes are known – on his Paris apartment block roof five years ago. The urban rooftop space above the exhibition hall is somewhat bigger: 14,000 square metres and almost exactly the size of a couple of football pitches. Already, the team of young urban farmers who tend it have picked, in one day, 3,000 lettuces and 150 punnets of strawberries. When the remaining two thirds of the vast open area are in production, 20 staff will harvest up to 1,000 kg of perhaps 35 different varieties of fruit and vegetables, every day. 'We're not ever, obviously, going to feed the whole city this way,' cautions Hardy. 'In the urban environment you're working with very significant practical constraints, clearly, on what you can do and where. But if enough unused space can be developed like this, there's no reason why you shouldn't eventually target maybe between 5% and 10% of consumption.'



Perhaps most significantly, however, this is a real-life showcase for the work of Hardy's flourishing urban agriculture consultancy, Agripolis, which is currently fielding enquiries from around the world to design, build and equip a new breed of soil-free inner-city farm. 'The method's advantages are many,' he says. 'First, I don't much like the fact that most of the fruit and vegetables we eat have been treated with something like 17 different pesticides, or that the intensive farming techniques that produced them are such huge generators of greenhouse gases. I don't much like the fact, either, that they've travelled an average of 2,000 refrigerated kilometres to my plate, that their quality is so poor, because the varieties are selected for their capacity to withstand such substantial journeys, or that 80% of the price I pay goes to wholesalers and transport companies, not the producers.'



Produce grown using this soil-free method, on the other hand – which relies solely on a small quantity of water, enriched with organic nutrients, pumped around a closed circuit of pipes, towers and trays – is 'produced up here, and sold locally, just down there. It barely travels at all,' Hardy says. 'You can select crop varieties for their flavour, not their resistance to the transport and storage chain, and you can pick them when they're really at their best, and not before.' No soil is exhausted, and the water that gently showers the plants' roots every 12 minutes is recycled, so the method uses 90% less water than a classic intensive farm for the same yield.



Urban farming is not, of course, a new phenomenon. Inner-city agriculture is booming from Shanghai to Detroit and Tokyo to Bangkok.



Strawberries are being grown in disused shipping containers, mushrooms in underground carparks. Aeroponic farming, he says, is 'virtuous'. The equipment weighs little, can be installed on almost any flat surface and is cheap to buy: roughly €100 to €150 per square metre. It is cheap to run, too, consuming a tiny fraction of the electricity used by some techniques.



Produce grown this way typically sells at prices that, while generally higher than those of classic intensive agriculture, are lower than soil-based organic growers. There are limits to what farmers can grow this way, of course, and much of the produce is suited to the summer months. 'Root vegetables we cannot do, at least not yet,' he says. 'Radishes are OK, but carrots, potatoes, that kind of thing – the roots are simply too long. Fruit trees are obviously not an option. And beans tend to take up a lot of space for not much return.' Nevertheless, urban farming of the kind being practised in Paris is one part of a bigger and fast-changing picture that is bringing food production closer to our lives.

When the remaining two thirds of the vast open area are in production, 20 staff will harvest up to 1,000 kg of perhaps 35 different varieties of fruit and vegetables, every day. 'We're not ever, obviously, going to feed the whole city this way,' cautions Hardy. 'In the urban environment you're working with very significant practical constraints, clearly, on what you can do and where. But if enough unused space can be developed like this, there's no reason why you shouldn't eventually target maybe between 5% and 10% of consumption.'



Perhaps most significantly, however, this is a real-life showcase for the work of Hardy's flourishing urban agriculture consultancy, Agripolis, which is currently fielding enquiries from around the world to design, build and equip a new breed of soil-free inner-city farm. 'The method's advantages are many,' he says. 'First, I don't much like the fact that most of the fruit and vegetables we eat have been treated with something like 17 different pesticides, or that the intensive farming techniques that produced them are such huge generators of greenhouse gases. I don't much like the fact, either, that they've travelled an average of 2,000 refrigerated kilometres to my plate, that their quality is so poor, because the varieties are selected for their capacity to withstand such substantial journeys, or that 80% of the price I pay goes to wholesalers and transport companies, not the producers.'



Produce grown using this soil-free method, on the other hand – which relies solely on a small quantity of water, enriched with organic nutrients, pumped around a closed circuit of pipes, towers and trays – is 'produced up here, and sold locally, just down there. It barely travels at all,' Hardy says. 'You can select crop varieties for their flavour, not their resistance to the transport and storage chain, and you can pick them when they're really at their best, and not before.' No soil is exhausted, and the water that gently showers the plants' roots every 12 minutes is recycled, so the method uses 90% less water than a classic intensive farm for the same yield.



Urban farming is not, of course, a new phenomenon. Inner-city agriculture is booming from Shanghai to Detroit and Tokyo to Bangkok.



Strawberries are being grown in disused shipping containers, mushrooms in underground carparks. Aeroponic farming, he says, is 'virtuous'. The equipment weighs little, can be installed on almost any flat surface and is cheap to buy: roughly €100 to €150 per square metre. It is cheap to run, too, consuming a tiny fraction of the electricity used by some techniques.



Produce grown this way typically sells at prices that, while generally higher than those of classic intensive agriculture, are lower than soil-based organic growers. There are limits to what farmers can grow this way, of course, and much of the produce is suited to the summer months. 'Root vegetables we cannot do, at least not yet,' he says. 'Radishes are OK, but carrots, potatoes, that kind of thing – the roots are simply too long. Fruit trees are obviously not an option. And beans tend to take up a lot of space for not much return.' Nevertheless, urban farming of the kind being practised in Paris is one part of a bigger and fast-changing picture that is bringing food production closer to our lives.

obj.title: Urban farming
obj.content:
[i]In Paris, urban farmers are trying a soil-free approach to agriculture that uses less space and fewer resources. Could it help cities face the threats to our food supplies?[/i][br/][br/]
On top of a striking new exhibition hall in southern Paris, the world's largest urban rooftop farm has started to bear fruit. Strawberries that are small, intensely flavoured and resplendently red sprout abundantly from large plastic tubes. Peer inside and you see the tubes are completely hollow, the roots of dozens of strawberry plants dangling down inside them. From identical vertical tubes nearby burst row upon row of lettuces; near those are aromatic herbs, such as basil, sage and peppermint. Opposite, in narrow, horizontal trays packed not with soil but with coconut fibre, grow cherry tomatoes, shiny aubergines and brightly coloured chards.[br/][br/]
Pascal Hardy, an engineer and sustainable development consultant, began experimenting with vertical farming and aeroponic growing towers – as the soil-free plastic tubes are known – on his Paris apartment block roof five years ago. The urban rooftop space above the exhibition hall is somewhat bigger: 14,000 square metres and almost exactly the size of a couple of football pitches. Already, the team of young urban farmers who tend it have picked, in one day, 3,000 lettuces and 150 punnets of strawberries. When the remaining two thirds of the vast open area are in production, 20 staff will harvest up to 1,000 kg of perhaps 35 different varieties of fruit and vegetables, every day. 'We're not ever, obviously, going to feed the whole city this way,' cautions Hardy. 'In the urban environment you're working with very significant practical constraints, clearly, on what you can do and where. But if enough unused space can be developed like this, there's no reason why you shouldn't eventually target maybe between 5% and 10% of consumption.'[br/][br/]
Perhaps most significantly, however, this is a real-life showcase for the work of Hardy's flourishing urban agriculture consultancy, Agripolis, which is currently fielding enquiries from around the world to design, build and equip a new breed of soil-free inner-city farm. 'The method's advantages are many,' he says. 'First, I don't much like the fact that most of the fruit and vegetables we eat have been treated with something like 17 different pesticides, or that the intensive farming techniques that produced them are such huge generators of greenhouse gases. I don't much like the fact, either, that they've travelled an average of 2,000 refrigerated kilometres to my plate, that their quality is so poor, because the varieties are selected for their capacity to withstand such substantial journeys, or that 80% of the price I pay goes to wholesalers and transport companies, not the producers.'[br/][br/]
Produce grown using this soil-free method, on the other hand – which relies solely on a small quantity of water, enriched with organic nutrients, pumped around a closed circuit of pipes, towers and trays – is 'produced up here, and sold locally, just down there. It barely travels at all,' Hardy says. 'You can select crop varieties for their flavour, not their resistance to the transport and storage chain, and you can pick them when they're really at their best, and not before.' No soil is exhausted, and the water that gently showers the plants' roots every 12 minutes is recycled, so the method uses 90% less water than a classic intensive farm for the same yield.[br/][br/]
Urban farming is not, of course, a new phenomenon. Inner-city agriculture is booming from Shanghai to Detroit and Tokyo to Bangkok.[br/][br/]
Strawberries are being grown in disused shipping containers, mushrooms in underground carparks. Aeroponic farming, he says, is 'virtuous'. The equipment weighs little, can be installed on almost any flat surface and is cheap to buy: roughly €100 to €150 per square metre. It is cheap to run, too, consuming a tiny fraction of the electricity used by some techniques.[br/][br/]
Produce grown this way typically sells at prices that, while generally higher than those of classic intensive agriculture, are lower than soil-based organic growers. There are limits to what farmers can grow this way, of course, and much of the produce is suited to the summer months. 'Root vegetables we cannot do, at least not yet,' he says. 'Radishes are OK, but carrots, potatoes, that kind of thing – the roots are simply too long. Fruit trees are obviously not an option. And beans tend to take up a lot of space for not much return.' Nevertheless, urban farming of the kind being practised in Paris is one part of a bigger and fast-changing picture that is bringing food production closer to our lives.[br/][br/]
obj.content_zh:
[i]在巴黎，城市农民正在尝试一种使用更少空间和资源的无土农业方法。它能帮助城市应对粮食供应面临的威胁吗？[/i][br/][br/]
在巴黎南部一座引人注目的新展览馆顶部，世界上最大的城市屋顶农场已经开始开花结果。小巧玲珑、味道浓郁、鲜红欲滴的草莓从大型塑料管中大量萌发。往里一看，管子完全是空心的，几十株草莓的根系垂在里面。旁边同样的垂直管子里，一排排莴苣破土而出；附近还有罗勒、鼠尾草和薄荷等芳香草本植物。对面狭窄的水平托盘里装的不是泥土，而是椰子纤维，里面种着樱桃番茄、闪亮的茄子和色彩鲜艳的甜菜。[br/][br/]
P帕斯卡尔-哈迪（Pascal Hardy）是一名工程师和可持续发展顾问，五年前他开始在自己的巴黎公寓楼屋顶上试验垂直耕作和气培种植塔（无土塑料管）。展览馆上方的城市屋顶空间更大一些：14,000 平方米，几乎相当于几个足球场大小。由城市青年农民组成的团队已经在一天之内采摘了 3000 株生菜和 150 蒲式耳草莓。当大片空地的其余三分之二投入生产后，20 名员工每天将收获多达 1000 公斤、约 35 个不同品种的水果和蔬菜。哈迪警告说："显然，我们不可能用这种方式养活整个城市。在城市环境中，你所能做的、能在哪里做，显然都受到很大的实际限制。但如果能像这样开发足够多的闲置空间，就没有理由不最终将目标锁定在 5%-10%的消耗量上。[br/][br/]
不过，最重要的是，这可能是哈迪的城市农业咨询公司 Agripolis 工作的真实展示，该公司目前正在接受来自世界各地的咨询，要求设计、建造和装备新型的无土市内农场。这种方法有很多优点，"他说。首先，我不太喜欢我们吃的大多数水果和蔬菜都经过大约 17 种不同杀虫剂的处理，也不太喜欢生产这些水果和蔬菜的集约化耕作技术会产生大量温室气体。我也不喜欢这些水果和蔬菜平均要经过 2000 公里的冷藏运输才能送到我的餐桌上，我也不喜欢它们的质量如此之差，因为这些品种是为了能够承受如此长途的运输而被挑选出来的，我也不喜欢我所支付的价格中有 80% 是批发商和运输公司的，而不是生产商的。[br/][br/]
另一方面，使用这种无土栽培法种植的农产品--仅靠少量富含有机养分的水在管道、塔架和托盘组成的闭合回路中循环--"在这里生产，在当地销售，就在下面。哈迪说："这些水几乎不经过任何运输。你可以根据作物的风味，而不是它们对运输和储存链的耐受性来选择作物品种，你可以在它们真正达到最佳状态时采摘，而不是在采摘之前。这种方法不会耗尽土壤，而且每 12 分钟轻轻喷洒在植物根部的水都会被循环利用，因此在产量相同的情况下，这种方法比传统的集约型农场少用 90% 的水。[br/][br/]
当然，城市农业并非新现象。从上海到底特律，从东京到曼谷，城市内的农业都在蓬勃发展。[br/][br/]
人们在废弃的集装箱里种植草莓，在地下停车场种植蘑菇。他说，气生栽培是 “良性 ”的。设备重量小，几乎可以安装在任何平面上，而且购买成本低：每平方米大约 100 至 150 欧元。它的运行成本也很低，耗电量仅为某些技术的一小部分。[br/][br/]
以这种方式种植的农产品的售价通常高于传统的集约型农业，但低于以土壤为基础的有机种植者。当然，农民用这种方式种植的作物也有局限性，大部分农产品都适合在夏季种植。他说："我们不能种植根茎类蔬菜，至少现在还不行。萝卜还可以，但胡萝卜、土豆之类的根太长了。果树显然也不行。而豆类往往占用大量空间，收益却不高。尽管如此，在巴黎开展的这种城市耕作只是快速变化的大环境中的一部分，它让粮食生产更加贴近我们的生活。
obj.html_content:
In Paris, urban farmers are trying a soil-free approach to agriculture that uses less space and fewer resources. Could it help cities face the threats to our food supplies?



On top of a striking new exhibition hall in southern Paris, the world's largest urban rooftop farm has started to bear fruit. Strawberries that are small, intensely flavoured and resplendently red sprout abundantly from large plastic tubes. Peer inside and you see the tubes are completely hollow, the roots of dozens of strawberry plants dangling down inside them. From identical vertical tubes nearby burst row upon row of lettuces; near those are aromatic herbs, such as basil, sage and peppermint. Opposite, in narrow, horizontal trays packed not with soil but with coconut fibre, grow cherry tomatoes, shiny aubergines and brightly coloured chards.



Pascal Hardy, an engineer and sustainable development consultant, began experimenting with vertical farming and aeroponic growing towers – as the soil-free plastic tubes are known – on his Paris apartment block roof five years ago. The urban rooftop space above the exhibition hall is somewhat bigger: 14,000 square metres and almost exactly the size of a couple of football pitches. Already, the team of young urban farmers who tend it have picked, in one day, 3,000 lettuces and 150 punnets of strawberries. When the remaining two thirds of the vast open area are in production, 20 staff will harvest up to 1,000 kg of perhaps 35 different varieties of fruit and vegetables, every day. 'We're not ever, obviously, going to feed the whole city this way,' cautions Hardy. 'In the urban environment you're working with very significant practical constraints, clearly, on what you can do and where. But if enough unused space can be developed like this, there's no reason why you shouldn't eventually target maybe between 5% and 10% of consumption.'



Perhaps most significantly, however, this is a real-life showcase for the work of Hardy's flourishing urban agriculture consultancy, Agripolis, which is currently fielding enquiries from around the world to design, build and equip a new breed of soil-free inner-city farm. 'The method's advantages are many,' he says. 'First, I don't much like the fact that most of the fruit and vegetables we eat have been treated with something like 17 different pesticides, or that the intensive farming techniques that produced them are such huge generators of greenhouse gases. I don't much like the fact, either, that they've travelled an average of 2,000 refrigerated kilometres to my plate, that their quality is so poor, because the varieties are selected for their capacity to withstand such substantial journeys, or that 80% of the price I pay goes to wholesalers and transport companies, not the producers.'



Produce grown using this soil-free method, on the other hand – which relies solely on a small quantity of water, enriched with organic nutrients, pumped around a closed circuit of pipes, towers and trays – is 'produced up here, and sold locally, just down there. It barely travels at all,' Hardy says. 'You can select crop varieties for their flavour, not their resistance to the transport and storage chain, and you can pick them when they're really at their best, and not before.' No soil is exhausted, and the water that gently showers the plants' roots every 12 minutes is recycled, so the method uses 90% less water than a classic intensive farm for the same yield.



Urban farming is not, of course, a new phenomenon. Inner-city agriculture is booming from Shanghai to Detroit and Tokyo to Bangkok.



Strawberries are being grown in disused shipping containers, mushrooms in underground carparks. Aeroponic farming, he says, is 'virtuous'. The equipment weighs little, can be installed on almost any flat surface and is cheap to buy: roughly €100 to €150 per square metre. It is cheap to run, too, consuming a tiny fraction of the electricity used by some techniques.



Produce grown this way typically sells at prices that, while generally higher than those of classic intensive agriculture, are lower than soil-based organic growers. There are limits to what farmers can grow this way, of course, and much of the produce is suited to the summer months. 'Root vegetables we cannot do, at least not yet,' he says. 'Radishes are OK, but carrots, potatoes, that kind of thing – the roots are simply too long. Fruit trees are obviously not an option. And beans tend to take up a lot of space for not much return.' Nevertheless, urban farming of the kind being practised in Paris is one part of a bigger and fast-changing picture that is bringing food production closer to our lives.

officialExplain.explain: 阅读题干可知，空格中所需答案有两个与之形成并列关系的植物名词提示：“草莓”和“香草”，因此在原文中答案词必定会出现在这二者附近，且必定也是某种农作物。用这个思路很快便能在文中第一段第二句里找到第一个线索“草莓”。不过本题略挑逗读者的耐心，因为第二句和第三句都是在极尽细节地描述草莓本身，没有提到其他植物。考生需连看三句，才能从本段第四句中梳理出另外两种植物，一种是题干给出的herbs，另外一种当然就是空格中所需的答案lettuces。
仔细阅读题干可知，空格内需要的必定是一个数字再加上一个重量单位，这对于寻找本题答案有着非常大的提示作用。考生如果能紧抓这条线索，就可以快捷高效地锁定第二段里第一次出现符合这个标准的句子。再用题干中的其他定位信息，例如daily和as much as来进行二次确认，则可通过句子中同义替换的every day和up to更加笃定自己的选择，进而得出答案为1000 kg。

本题的定位十分简单，直接凭借原样重现的数字提示10%即可锁定原文位置。不过这句话本身其实并没有与题干中所说的city或者overall产生直接关联，所以考生与其直接定位、只看此句，不如按照平行阅读的思路依次顺序阅读文段、获得完整的上下文信息，即可知上一句说的是“光靠无土栽培农产品可养活不了整个城市”，于是顺理成章地明白这句话是在表示“但是满足全部所需中的5%~10%还是很有希望的”，由此可以笃定答案为(food) consumption。

考生在解答表格类填空题时，除了要耐心阅读空格句本身之外，也不妨观察一下对应位置的表头信息，对定位和解题也都是有帮助的。例如表头中的Growth提示的就是intensive farming这种传统集约农业方法在“种植”本身的过程中用了各种各样的什么。考生还需注意，同在这一栏中还有另一个信息：techniques pollute air。也就是说，原文中描述破坏空气环境的信息是不能填在本题空格内的。遵循这个思路回原文定位对比，即可得出答案为pesticides。

本题的定位相比本篇大多数其他填空题来说要略微曲折一点，因为两个关键的定位线索词survive和long在原文中分别被替换成了withstand和substantial；不过由于这些词本身都是长年累月高频出现在剑桥真题中的“常客”，所以若说定位困难，那倒也不至于。考生只需自身基础词汇量过关，则应当能顺利找到定位，得出答案为journeys。本题对应原文句的结构与上一题的定位句几乎一模一样：I don’t like the fact that…, that…, or that…，三个that后面分别给出“I”不喜欢看到的三种状况。
本题与上一题来自同一个原文句，虽然同义替换的方式比较曲折委婉，说“80%的收入都去了批发商和运输公司的口袋里，而不是给到生产者们”，言下之意后者拿的少；但是这个表述实在谈不上难懂，因而考生应当可以顺利理解并轻松得出答案为producers。
本题归根到底不过是考查考生对chosen（choose的过去分词形式）和select这两个基础词汇的准确认知。有了这个基本储备，考生就应当可以在阅读到相应原文句时，看出在这种新型气培法农业操作中，选择种什么可以是出于对其滋味的选择，而非看其是否耐长途运输和存储，由此可得出答案为flavour。

其实从本文开篇处，我们就已经知道了很多农作物是种植在屋顶上的，因此题干中的above ground这个信息其实不难确认。于是考生所面临的主要问题是：在below ground的位置是否也可以进行无土栽培？由于在解答前面两个填空题型时，文章中始终没有出现过与“种在地面以下”相关的任何信息，所以解题至此，考生已经基本可以确认判断题的全部答案都应集中在文章的最后两个自然段中，就更容易沉住气、细心阅读相应内容，继而通过原文中的underground carparks确认答案为TRUE。
考生在读到第五段第四句时，不难看到equipment这个题干词；但仔细阅读后就会发现，无论是此句还是之后的几句，描述的都是这些设备“轻便、好安装、用起来不贵”等优点，却完全没有提及题干所关注的“是否可以手工制作”这一点，由此可得答案应为NOT GIVEN。
由于上一道题的信息在原文中未给出，考生要确认本题答案则必然需要耐心多看几句才更有把握，因此在这个阅读确认的过程中，应当不难看到本题的定位句。这句话本身也不难理解，直白道出了这种农业方法只用一点点电力，比一些其他技术省电多了，这与题干表述形成了非常明确的矛盾抵触，因此答案应为FALSE。

本题考查考生关注细节的精准度，需格外注意题干中与“都市无土栽培作物”进行价格比较的对象是“传统有机农产品”而非“传统农产品”。后者使用的是最原始的传统种植方法，而前者则是前些年开始新兴的“用有机方法种植出来的作物”。能准确意识到这一点的考生，就能在接下来对原文对应句的阅读理解中看到：这种最新的无土栽培作物比传统老办法种出来的作物要贵一些，但是比先前那种有机方法所产出的菜蔬却便宜一些。这与题干描述相一致，因此答案应为TRUE。
本题的定位紧邻上一题，且考生看到本句开头处的limits时，其实就已经可以收到一些隐约提示：接下来恐怕要提及这种无土栽培方法的缺点了。继续读下去会发现果不其然：大多数作物只适合the summer months，这与题干的意思相矛盾，可知答案应为FALSE。
题干中的beans倒是一模一样地出现在了全文的倒数第二句话里，但是文中给出的缺点在于“占空间、回报少”，至于“生长速度快慢与否”，直到全文结束也没有任何提及，由此可以笃定答案应为NOT GIVEN。
